ICMP is for pinging, it's not a navigator.

questions.question: Silicon is getting nervous. Which class of materials, featuring a specific cubic crystal structure, is currently the darling of solar research due to its low cost and high efficiency potential?
questions.explanation: Perovskites are materials with a specific crystal structure that are highly efficient at absorbing light. They can be printed using low-cost techniques, unlike high-purity silicon, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Silicon is getting worried. Which class of materials is the current darling of solar research for its potential?
presentation.explanationStyled: Perovskites. They’re cheap and efficient. Unlike the current curriculum, they might actually have a future.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Solar energy is great, but it won't fix my workload.
wrongAnswerQuips: Fullerenes are just fancy soccer ball molecules.
Gallium Arsenide is far too expensive for solar.
Cadmium Telluride is old news in solar.

questions.question: In his 1986 book 'Engines of Creation,' Eric Drexler warned of what apocalyptic scenario where self-replicating nanobots reduce all biological life to a mass of identical gray matter?
questions.explanation: The 'Grey Goo' scenario is a hypothetical catastrophe where out-of-control self-replicating molecular machines consume all biomass on Earth while replicating themselves, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Eric Drexler warned of what apocalyptic scenario where self-replicating nanobots turn everything into useless matter?
presentation.explanationStyled: Grey Goo. The idea that everything will eventually be reduced to a pile of identical nothingness. Sounds like my inbox on a Monday.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Nanobots sound like a nightmare, but at least they're small.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Singularity is just robots getting smarter.
Technological Tsunami, a bit dramatic, don't you think?
Digital Blight, sounds like a cheap sci-fi movie.

questions.question: Golden Rice was bioengineered to synthesize which specific precursor to Vitamin A in its endosperm, aiming to fix the nutritional deficiencies of a billion people?
questions.explanation: Golden Rice was modified to produce beta-carotene (which gives it the yellow color). When consumed, the body converts this into Vitamin A, preventing blindness and immune deficiency, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Golden Rice was bioengineered to synthesize which precursor to Vitamin A to fix nutritional deficiencies?
presentation.explanationStyled: Beta-carotene. It gives the rice its yellow color. It's a nice attempt to fix the world, but it doesn't help my nutritional deficiency of caffeine.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Engineering food is a bit much, but it has good intentions.
wrongAnswerQuips: Lycopene is for tomatoes, not for rice.
Zeaxanthin is for eyes, not a vitamin precursor.
Anthocyanin is just for purple plants.

questions.question: Most modern encryption depends on the fact that factoring large numbers is really, really hard. Which 1994 algorithm proved that a quantum computer could do it in polynomial time?
questions.explanation: Peter Shor's algorithm can find the prime factors of an integer significantly faster than any known classical algorithm, theoretically rendering RSA encryption obsolete, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Most encryption relies on factoring being hard. Which 1994 algorithm proved a quantum computer could do it easily?
presentation.explanationStyled: Shor's Algorithm. It makes current encryption look like a paper lock. I'm sure it'll make my grading security even worse.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Quantum computing is just a way to make everything insecure.
wrongAnswerQuips: Grover's is for searching, not for factoring.
Deutsch-Jozsa is about oracle functions, move on.
Simon's Algorithm, nice try, but wrong scope.

questions.question: Before your phone did it for free, you needed a dedicated brick. In 1988, which device became the first commercially available handheld GPS receiver?
questions.explanation: The Magellan NAV 1000 was the first commercial handheld unit. It was heavy, cost about $3,000, and could only calculate a position after staring at the sky for several minutes, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Before phones had free GPS, we had bricks. What was the name of the first commercial handheld GPS from 1988?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Magellan NAV 1000. It was heavy, slow, and expensive. It was the perfect metaphor for the technology I'm forced to use.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Technology has come a long way, but it's still all bricks.
wrongAnswerQuips: Garmin came later, stop assuming brands.
Trimble Scout, sounds like a boy scout project.
Sony Pyxis, way too modern for the 80s.

questions.question: Intel's 2007 transition to the 45nm node was a major engineering hurdle. What material did they adopt to replace silicon dioxide as the gate dielectric to stop electrons from leaking out?
questions.explanation: By replacing silicon dioxide with a hafnium-based 'high-k' material, Intel was able to reduce gate leakage current by over 100 times, allowing for further miniaturization, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: When Intel went to 45nm, they needed a new dielectric to stop electrons from leaking. What did they use?
presentation.explanationStyled: A Hafnium-based high-k dielectric. It stopped the leaks. I wish I could stop the leaks in my classroom morale with a simple coating.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Semiconductors are exhausting to explain, so pay attention.
wrongAnswerQuips: Barium is for batteries, not for gate dielectrics.
Gallium Nitride is for power, not for logic.
Silicon Carbide is for high heat environments.

questions.question: Morton Heilig's 1962 'Sensorama' wasn't just a screen. It was a bulky mechanical cabinet that stimulated five senses. Which of these was a feature of the Sensorama experience?
questions.explanation: The Sensorama was an early 'multisensory' machine that used stereoscopic 3D images, fans for wind, a vibrating seat, and chemical 'smell' emitters to simulate reality, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Morton Heilig’s 1962 'Sensorama' was a mechanical cabinet meant to stimulate five senses. Which feature did it have?
presentation.explanationStyled: It had odor emitters. It tried to simulate reality, but it’s basically just a fancy chair with smell. Much like my classroom experience.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Virtual reality is older than you think, and twice as annoying.
wrongAnswerQuips: Haptic gloves weren't that advanced yet.
Neural electrodes? Keep it out of my brain.
Voice recognition? Not in 1962, dream on.

questions.question: Long before Amazon or Google, which computer scientist famously predicted in 1961 that 'computation may someday be organized as a public utility' like water or electricity?
questions.explanation: John McCarthy, the father of Lisp and AI, was a major advocate for 'utility computing' and time-sharing, which are the fundamental conceptual ancestors of modern cloud computing, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Which computer scientist predicted in 1961 that computation would be a public utility like water or electricity?
presentation.explanationStyled: John McCarthy. He was the one who saw it all coming. Utility computing: because why have your own hardware when you can pay someone else to crash it?
presentation.hostLeadIn: Utility computing—because everything has to be a service now.
wrongAnswerQuips: Licklider was focused on the network, not utility.
Engelbart was too busy with his mouse.
Vint Cerf was just starting his work.

questions.question: In 1747, Royal Navy surgeon James Lind conducted what is cited as the first clinical trial on the HMS Salisbury; while he tested six different 'treatments,' which fruit did he actually prove effective against scurvy?
questions.explanation: Lind's experiment compared cider, vitriol, vinegar, seawater, oranges, and lemons. While the British Navy later famously switched to limes for cost reasons (earning sailors the nickname 'Limeys'), Lind's original success was specifically with lemons and oranges.

presentation.questionStyled: In 1747, Royal Navy surgeon James Lind ran the world's first clinical trial aboard the HMS Salisbury. He tested six different 'cures' for scurvy, but which fruit actually saved the crew?
presentation.explanationStyled: Lind pitted cider, seawater, and acids against oranges and lemons. The British Navy later switched to limes—creating the 'Limey' moniker—but those first lemons and oranges were the real winners. I wish my lesson plans worked that cleanly.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Back in my day, we didn't have scurvy, just failing grades.
wrongAnswerQuips: Limes were a later cost-cutting blunder, sorry.
That's just a salad, not medicine.
Good luck curing scurvy with fancy cider.

questions.question: Since the 1764 invention of the Spinning Jenny apparently wasn't fast enough for the British textile industry, James Hargreaves designed it to allow a single worker to operate how many spindles simultaneously in its initial model?
questions.explanation: The original Spinning Jenny operated eight spindles at once. Later versions increased this number significantly, which terrified local spinners enough to lead them to break into Hargreaves' house and destroy his machines.

presentation.questionStyled: Since the 1764 Spinning Jenny apparently didn't automate misery fast enough for the textile industry, how many spindles did James Hargreaves' initial model operate at once?
presentation.explanationStyled: The original model handled eight spindles. Once the machines got bigger and faster, the local workers did what any reasonable person would do: they broke into his house and smashed the whole thing. I sympathize.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Nothing says 'progress' like losing your job to a machine.
wrongAnswerQuips: Not fast enough, try again.
That would be far too productive for 1764.
One hundred? Keep dreaming.

questions.question: If the 'Lost Colony' of Roanoke actually wanted to be found in 1590, they left a cryptic mess; while 'CROATOAN' was on a post, what three letters were found carved into a nearby tree?
questions.explanation: John White found the letters 'CRO' carved into a tree on the bluff and the full word 'CROATOAN' on a palisade post. These were the only clues left behind by the 115 settlers, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: If the 'Lost Colony' of Roanoke actually wanted to be found in 1590, they did a terrible job. While 'CROATOAN' was on a post, what three letters were on a nearby tree?
presentation.explanationStyled: John White found CRO carved into the tree. Just three letters. No map, no note, just a vague hint that’s been bothering historians for centuries. I’m not mad, just disappointed they couldn't leave a proper forwarding address.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Even my students leave clearer notes when they skip class.
wrongAnswerQuips: They weren't that desperate for help.
Too modern, and honestly, a bit cliché.
Like they were going to leave a 'Lost' label.

questions.question: The 751 AD Battle of Talas didn't just decide the border between the Abbasids and the Tang; it allegedly involved the capture of two specific Chinese prisoners who leaked the secret of which industry to the Islamic world?
questions.explanation: Legend states that among the prisoners taken at the Battle of Talas were skilled papermakers, which led to the first paper mills in Samarkand and the subsequent intellectual boom in the Islamic Golden Age.

presentation.questionStyled: The 751 AD Battle of Talas changed history by capturing Chinese prisoners who leaked the secret of which industry to the Islamic world?
presentation.explanationStyled: Legend says the prisoners brought the secrets of papermaking to Samarkand, igniting the Islamic Golden Age. It’s amazing how much one technology can change everything—I wish the photocopier in the breakroom worked that well.
presentation.hostLeadIn: War is mostly just people fighting over who gets the stationery.
wrongAnswerQuips: Silk was the prize, not the industry leaked.
Gunpowder was messy enough as it was.
They were busy enough without making porcelain.

questions.question: Apparently tired of Western control, Gamal Abdel Nasser sparked the 1956 Suez Crisis by nationalizing the canal primarily to fund the construction of what massive infrastructure project?
questions.explanation: Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal to collect the tolls to pay for the Aswan High Dam after the United States and Britain withdrew their offers of funding, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal in 1956 to stick it to the West, but what massive project did he actually need the money for?
presentation.explanationStyled: He wanted the Aswan High Dam. When the U.S. and Britain pulled the funding, he grabbed the canal's tolls to pay for it himself. It’s almost impressive how much trouble one dam can cause.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Nasser really knew how to throw a tantrum on a geopolitical scale.
wrongAnswerQuips: The river wasn't going to build itself.
A bit too early for a metro, don't you think?
Building a highway through the Sinai? Please.

questions.question: Johannes Gutenberg didn't just stumble onto the printing press; he spent years mastering which professional craft that gave him the necessary metallurgy skills to create 'movable type'?
questions.explanation: Gutenberg was trained as a goldsmith, which allowed him to understand the precise alloys and casting techniques required to make durable metal type that wouldn't melt under the pressure of a press, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Johannes Gutenberg didn't just wake up one day and invent the printing press. He spent years honing his skills in which profession to master the metallurgy for movable type?
presentation.explanationStyled: He was a goldsmith. That precision work with alloys and casting is exactly why his metal type didn't melt under pressure. It's almost like learning the fundamentals actually matters, imagine that.
presentation.hostLeadIn: If he had just paid attention in shop class, who knows?
wrongAnswerQuips: Blacksmithing is too blunt for such tiny work.
Clockmaking is close, but no cigar.
Armoring is far too heavy for printing.

questions.question: The Mary Rose was one of Henry VIII's favorite toys until it sank in 1545; it is historically notable for being one of the first English ships specifically built to execute which naval tactic?
choices: Aerial spotting from the crow's nest
questions.explanation: The Mary Rose was among the first vessels to feature a hull designed with integrated gunports, allowing it to carry heavy bronze cannons low in the ship for broadside volleys, rather than just small anti-personnel guns.

presentation.questionStyled: The Mary Rose was one of Henry VIII's favorite toys before it sank in 1545. Why was it a game-changer for naval combat?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was one of the first built for broadside fire through gunports. Henry finally got to fire his big toys out of the sides of the ship instead of just the ends. I'm sure it was very expensive.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Another expensive lesson in how not to build a boat.
wrongAnswerQuips: Ramming is such a prehistoric tactic.
Not even the Wright brothers were that bold.
Steam engines hadn't ruined the ocean yet.

questions.question: If you were a Luddite in 1811, you weren't just 'bad with computers'; you were likely a skilled weaver following the orders of which mythical, possibly non-existent leader to smash knitting frames?
questions.explanation: The Luddites claimed to be followers of 'Ned Ludd' (or General Ludd), a folkloric figure who allegedly broke two knitting frames in a fit of rage in 1779. There is no evidence he actually existed, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: If you were a Luddite in 1811, you weren't just 'bad with computers'; you were a weaver following which mythical leader to smash knitting frames?
presentation.explanationStyled: They worshipped General Ned Ludd, a guy who probably didn't exist, but it made smashing machines feel like a noble cause. I feel that kind of illogical rebellion every time the bell rings.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Imagine creating a fake boss just to complain about technology.
wrongAnswerQuips: Captain Swing was busy burning haystacks.
Jack Cade was a medieval populist, get current.
Paine was too busy writing pamphlets for this.

questions.question: The Mayflower Compact wasn't an act of pure altruism; the Pilgrims only signed it because they had landed in the 'wrong' place and lacked a legal patent for which specific territory?
questions.explanation: The Pilgrims were intended for Virginia. Having landed at Cape Cod instead, some 'strangers' on board argued they were no longer under the King's patent, forcing the leaders to create their own social contract for self-governance.

presentation.questionStyled: The Mayflower Compact wasn't just 'good vibes'; the Pilgrims signed it because they were in the wrong place and lacked a patent for which territory?
presentation.explanationStyled: They aimed for Virginia but hit Cape Cod instead. Once they realized they were outside the jurisdiction, they had to invent their own rules to keep the peace. Very typical, going off-script and making a mess.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Navigation really wasn't a strong suit for the early settlers.
wrongAnswerQuips: They knew exactly where they were going, obviously.
Plymouth Rock wasn't even on the map yet.
New Amsterdam was already taken, obviously.

questions.question: The 1994 Rwandan Genocide was ignited when the plane carrying President Juvénal Habyarimana was shot down; what was the specific ethnicity of the majority Hutu leadership that orchestrated the subsequent massacres?
questions.explanation: While the assassination remains a subject of debate, Hutu extremists (Interahamwe) used the event as a pretext to launch a pre-planned genocide against the Tutsi minority and moderate Hutus, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1994 Rwandan Genocide was sparked by a plane crash, but what was the specific ethnicity of the majority leadership that planned the massacres?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Hutu extremist leadership orchestrated the slaughter of Tutsis and moderate Hutus. It’s a tragic part of history that gets glossed over far too often. I'm not mad, just disappointed in humanity.
presentation.hostLeadIn: History is full of people making terrible choices, isn't it?
wrongAnswerQuips: The Tutsi were the victims here.
Twa people weren't the ones in power.
Bantu is a broad family, not the answer.

questions.question: If you've ever been baffled by a curveball, you can thank which physical phenomenon that describes the lift generated by a spinning object moving through air?
questions.explanation: Named after Heinrich Gustav Magnus, this effect occurs because the spin of the ball creates a velocity differential in the air on opposite sides, resulting in a pressure difference that 'curves' the flight path.

presentation.questionStyled: Ever wonder why a curveball actually curves? What do we call that phenomenon where a spinning object generates lift?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s the Magnus Effect. The air moves faster on one side than the other, creating a pressure difference. Physics is just like a classroom—everyone is trying to get ahead, and some people just spin out of control.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Sports physics, because knowing why you lost is apparently important.
wrongAnswerQuips: Bernoulli is for pipes, not baseballs.
Euler was busy with much harder math.
That's just a number, not an effect.

questions.question: The Haber-Bosch process is the reason billions of people aren't starving right now, but it requires which specific catalyst to efficiently fix atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia?
questions.explanation: The process uses a heterogeneous catalyst, typically iron promoted with K2O, CaO, SiO2, and Al2O3, to break the incredibly strong triple bond of N2 gas under high pressure and temperature, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Haber-Bosch process keeps the planet fed, but it needs a specific catalyst to break down nitrogen. Which one does the trick?
presentation.explanationStyled: It uses iron with promoters. Without that catalyst, the nitrogen bond is way too strong to break. It’s a bit like trying to motivate my 10th graders without coffee—just not going to happen.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Chemistry is hard, eating is easy. You're welcome.
wrongAnswerQuips: Platinum is too expensive for making fertilizer.
Palladium is for cars, not your dinner.
Vanadium is for different reactions, nice try.

questions.question: In a classic example of a 'trophic cascade,' the 1995 reintroduction of which species to Yellowstone National Park literally changed the physical flow of the rivers?
questions.explanation: Wolves reduced the elk population and changed their behavior (preventing overgrazing near riverbanks), which allowed willow and aspen to return, stabilizing banks and reducing erosion, which changed the river paths.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1995 reintroduction of which species to Yellowstone caused a 'trophic cascade' so big it actually reshaped the rivers?
presentation.explanationStyled: The Gray Wolves. By hunting the elk, they forced them to move, allowing the willow and aspen to regrow, which stabilized the riverbanks. Everything is connected, and yet, nobody ever listens.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Who knew nature was just a giant, complicated group project?
wrongAnswerQuips: Beavers change dams, not entire river paths.
Grizzly bears just eat the berries.
Elk are the ones eating the trees.

questions.question: Jocelyn Bell Burnell found the first pulsar in 1967 and all she got was the nickname 'Little Green Men' while her supervisor got the Nobel. What was the specific designation of that first radio source?
questions.explanation: The source was designated CP 1919 (Cambridge Pulsar at RA 19h 19m). Bell Burnell noticed the 'bit of scruff' on the chart recorder that pulsed with incredible regularity, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Jocelyn Bell Burnell found the first pulsar in 1967, but it was labeled with what specific designation that initially confused everyone?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was CP 1919. She saw the 'little green men' signal, but it was actually a pulsar. Her supervisor got the Nobel, and she got a nickname. Ah, academia—the circle of life.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Another brilliant woman ignored by her boss. Groundbreaking.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's a different star system, try again.
SGR is for magnetars, stay focused.
Cygnus X-1 is a black hole, not a pulsar.

questions.question: While Joseph Priestley was busy heating mercuric oxide in 1774, he isolated a gas he called 'dephlogisticated air'; which element was he actually boring us with?
questions.explanation: Priestley discovered oxygen independently, though Lavoisier later gave it its modern name and debunked the 'phlogiston' theory that Priestley clung to for the rest of his life, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Joseph Priestley called his discovery 'dephlogisticated air' back in 1774. What element was he actually talking about?
presentation.explanationStyled: He found oxygen, but he was so obsessed with the 'phlogiston' theory that he couldn't quite see what he had. Kind of like a student who has the right answer but writes the wrong one anyway.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Naming things is clearly the hardest part of science.
wrongAnswerQuips: Hydrogen is explosive, Priestley wasn't that reckless.
Nitrogen is everywhere, you'd think he'd notice.
CO2 is for plants, try harder.

questions.question: The 1987 Montreal Protocol is often praised as the only environmental treaty that actually worked; it successfully phased out which class of chemicals that were eating a hole in the stratosphere?
questions.explanation: CFCs, used in refrigeration and aerosols, were found to release chlorine atoms that catalyze the destruction of ozone molecules. The treaty led to a significant recovery of the ozone layer, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1987 Montreal Protocol actually worked. What chemical class did it ban to stop us from burning a hole in the sky?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was CFCs. We actually agreed to stop using them and the ozone layer started healing. It’s the only time we didn't completely ruin a group project. I'm genuinely impressed.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Sometimes humanity passes the test. Rarely, but sometimes.
wrongAnswerQuips: HFCs were the failed replacement, try again.
PCBs are just for transformers, not the sky.
Nitrous oxide is for fast cars, not ozone.

questions.question: Henry Cavendish's 1798 experiment is often mislabeled as 'weighing the Earth,' but his torsion balance actually provided the first accurate measurement of which fundamental constant?
questions.explanation: By measuring the tiny gravitational attraction between lead spheres, Cavendish was able to calculate the Earth's density and, by extension, the value of 'Big G' in Newton's law of universal gravitation.

presentation.questionStyled: Henry Cavendish's 1798 experiment is famous for more than just 'weighing the Earth.' What fundamental constant did he actually measure?
presentation.explanationStyled: He measured the Gravitational Constant (G). By measuring the attraction between lead spheres, he finally gave us a real number for Newton's gravity. A bit more useful than most things I do all day.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Gravity is the only thing keeping me in this chair.
wrongAnswerQuips: Light speed is way too fast to measure that way.
Planck's constant is for atoms, look closer.
That's for quantum weirdness, not physics.

questions.question: Unless you're a fan of watching paint dry, you might not care that capillary action is driven by a competition between which two types of molecular forces?
questions.explanation: Capillary action occurs when adhesion (the attraction between the liquid and the container wall) is stronger than cohesion (the attraction between the liquid molecules themselves), in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: If you don't want to watch paint dry, you'd ignore capillary action. But what are the two molecular forces competing to make it happen?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s a fight between Adhesion and Cohesion. One wants to stick to the wall, the other wants to stick to itself. Much like my students when I ask them to pick partners for a lab.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Physics is just molecular drama, let's be honest.
wrongAnswerQuips: Gravity and friction are just for lazy physics.
Viscosity is just how thick the drama is.
Tension is just what I feel every Monday.

questions.question: The first industrial robot, Unimate, joined the General Motors workforce in 1961; what was the specific 'dirty' task it was designed to handle that human workers found too dangerous?
questions.explanation: Unimate was a 4,000-pound hydraulic arm that took red-hot die castings of door handles and other parts and welded them to auto bodies, protecting humans from toxic fumes and heat, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Unimate, the first industrial robot, joined GM in 1961. What 'dirty' task was it designed to handle?
presentation.explanationStyled: It handled die casting and spot welding. Red-hot parts and toxic fumes—perfect for a robot that doesn't complain about the curriculum or its pension. Must be nice.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Finally, a coworker that doesn't ask for a reference.
wrongAnswerQuips: Wiring is for humans, robots have wires.
They have people for acid vats, sadly.
Tires aren't that dangerous, get real.

questions.question: Dmitri Mendeleev was so confident in his Periodic Table that he predicted the existence of 'eka-aluminum' years before it was discovered and named what?
questions.explanation: Mendeleev predicted Gallium's atomic weight, density, and melting point with startling accuracy, proving the validity of his periodic arrangement when it was discovered in 1875, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Dmitri Mendeleev was so sure of his Periodic Table that he predicted 'eka-aluminum' years before its discovery. What do we call it now?
presentation.explanationStyled: He called it Gallium. He was so accurate that he even guessed the density and melting point correctly. I wish my students were even half as prepared as his predictions.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Science is basically just being right before everyone else.
wrongAnswerQuips: Germanium was a different prediction, sorry.
Scandium was eka-boron, keep studying.
Indium was already known, try again.

questions.question: On May 22, 2010, Laszlo Hanyecz made the most expensive fast-food purchase in history by trading 10,000 BTC for two pizzas. Which chain actually fulfilled that order via a third-party intermediary?
questions.explanation: Laszlo posted on a forum offering 10,000 BTC for someone to order him two Papa John's pizzas. At today's prices, those pizzas would be worth hundreds of millions of dollars, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: In 2010, Laszlo Hanyecz spent 10,000 BTC on two pizzas. Which chain actually delivered them?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was Papa John's. Today, those pizzas would be worth more than this entire school district. I try not to think about it—it makes me want to scream into a pillow.
presentation.hostLeadIn: That's the most expensive lunch in history. I'm tired.
wrongAnswerQuips: Domino's would never accept that kind of payment.
Pizza Hut wasn't in on the joke.
Little Caesars only accepts actual money.

questions.question: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants are basically giant mirrors that cook salt; which specific combination of salts is most commonly used as the thermal storage medium?
questions.explanation: Commonly known as 'solar salt,' the 60/40 mix of sodium nitrate and potassium nitrate remains liquid at high temperatures and can store thermal energy for hours to generate steam after sunset, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Concentrated Solar Power plants are just mirrors cooking salt. What salt mix is the gold standard for storage?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s Sodium nitrate and Potassium nitrate. They call it 'solar salt.' It stores the heat so the plant can run after sunset. I wish I could store energy like that for my 2 PM lectures.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Mirror-cooked salt. Sounds like my attempt at a cafeteria lunch.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's just table salt, go back to school.
Lithium is for your phone, not the sun.
Potassium chloride is a salt substitute, not a battery.

questions.question: If you're worried about 'Grey Goo' turning the Earth into a giant pile of microscopic dust, you should probably blame which author of the 1986 book 'Engines of Creation'?
questions.explanation: Eric Drexler popularized the concept of molecular assemblers and the hypothetical 'Grey Goo' catastrophe where self-replicating nanobots consume all organic matter, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: If you're worried about 'Grey Goo' nanobots destroying the world, you can blame which author?
presentation.explanationStyled: Kim Eric Drexler, in his 1986 book 'Engines of Creation.' He made everyone terrified of microscopic robots eating the Earth. Like I don't have enough to worry about already.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Just what I needed, another reason to lose sleep.
wrongAnswerQuips: Stephenson writes about code, not gray goo.
Crichton writes about dinosaurs and viruses.
Asimov had rules, unlike these tiny monsters.

questions.question: BGP is essentially the 'glue' that holds the internet together by managing how packets cross Autonomous Systems. What does the acronym BGP actually stand for?
questions.explanation: The Border Gateway Protocol is a path-vector protocol that makes routing decisions based on paths, network policies, or rule-sets configured by network administrators, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The internet stays glued together thanks to BGP. What does that acronym actually stand for?
presentation.explanationStyled: Border Gateway Protocol. It manages how data moves between systems. It’s complicated, messy, and prone to breaking—pretty much the definition of my department meetings.
presentation.hostLeadIn: It's the glue holding the internet, yet I can't get Wi-Fi.
wrongAnswerQuips: Binary is for zeros, not borders.
Broadband global path? Sounds made up.
Base grid processor? Sounds like a printer.

questions.question: CRISPR-Cas9 is a set of 'molecular scissors' borrowed from a bacterial immune system; what does the 'R' in the acronym CRISPR stand for?
questions.explanation: CRISPR stands for Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats. It is used alongside the Cas9 protein to target and edit specific DNA sequences, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: CRISPR-Cas9 acts like molecular scissors. But what does the 'R' in CRISPR actually stand for?
presentation.explanationStyled: Repeats. Clustered Regularly Interspaced Short Palindromic Repeats. It's how bacteria store viral memories so they can cut up bad DNA. I wish I had a system that efficient for grading essays.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Biology is just coding for squishy stuff.
wrongAnswerQuips: Recombinant is for the finished project, not the tool.
Ribonucleic is for RNA, not this acronym.
Retroactive doesn't even make sense here.

questions.question: Moore's Law isn't a law of physics, it's just an observation that has been revised multiple times. In his 1975 update, Gordon Moore predicted the doubling of transistors would occur every how many months?
questions.explanation: Moore originally said every year in 1965, then updated it in 1975 to every two years (24 months). The common '18 months' figure is actually a combination of Moore's Law and David House's prediction about performance.

presentation.questionStyled: Moore's Law is less of a law and more of an observation. In 1975, how often did Gordon Moore say transistors would double?
presentation.explanationStyled: He updated it to 24 months. The '18 months' myth is just a mix-up with other industry specs. People love a snappy number, even if it's technically wrong. It’s like when kids just invent dates for their homework.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Facts are flexible if you try hard enough, I guess.
wrongAnswerQuips: That was his original 1965 guess.
18 months is a common mistake, keep guessing.
36 months is just too slow for tech.

questions.question: Long before the Oculus, Morton Heilig's 1962 'Sensorama' attempted to immerse users in a multi-sensory experience; which of these was NOT a feature of the device?
questions.explanation: The Sensorama was a mechanical cabinet that stimulated sight, sound, smell, and touch (via vibration and wind), but it did not include haptic gloves, which didn't appear until much later, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Morton Heilig's 1962 'Sensorama' was the grandfather of VR. Which of these wasn't a feature of that machine?
presentation.explanationStyled: It didn't have haptic gloves. It had smell, wind, and 3D, but touch was too advanced for the 60s. I can barely get my projector to show a slide, let alone recreate smell.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The 60s were weird, and this machine proves it.
wrongAnswerQuips: Stereoscopic 3D was the whole point.
It definitely had vibration, that's half the fun.
Scent emitters were its claim to fame.

questions.question: Amazon S3 basically runs half the modern internet; since naming things is hard, what do the three 'S's in S3 stand for?
questions.explanation: Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service) was launched in 2006 and provides object storage through a web service interface, becoming a core building block of AWS, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Amazon S3 runs half the internet. What do the three 'S's stand for?
presentation.explanationStyled: Simple Storage Service. It’s anything but simple, but that’s the name. I’ve reached the point where I stop questioning the naming conventions and just try to survive the day.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Naming things is clearly the weak point of tech giants.
wrongAnswerQuips: Secure Storage Solution? Too optimistic for Amazon.
Systemic Scaled Storage? Sounds too academic.
Standard Server Software? Too boring.

questions.question: In 1975, Kodak engineer Steven Sasson built the first digital camera, a toaster-sized monstrosity that took 23 seconds to record a single 0.01 megapixel image onto what medium?
questions.explanation: The prototype used a CCD sensor and digitized the signal to be recorded onto a standard Phillips cassette tape. To view the images, the data had to be read from the tape by a custom playback device, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Steven Sasson built the first digital camera in 1975. Where did he store the images?
presentation.explanationStyled: He used a cassette tape. It took 23 seconds to record a single grainy image. Honestly, sounds like the speed of my students' brains before their first coffee.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The first digital camera was basically a toaster.
wrongAnswerQuips: Floppy disks weren't that common yet.
Punch cards are too stone-age for photography.
Magnetic bubble memory was a pipe dream.

questions.question: Perovskite solar cells are the current darlings of the solar industry; the term 'Perovskite' actually refers to any material that shares the same what?
questions.explanation: Perovskite is a crystal structure (not a specific element) named after Lev Perovski. In solar tech, these materials can be manufactured cheaply and are rapidly approaching the efficiency of silicon, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Perovskite is the new solar energy darling. What does the term actually describe?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s a crystal structure (ABX3). It’s not one element; it's a specific pattern. Everything in nature is just geometry with a bad attitude. I think we have that in common.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Solar energy, because the current power bill is a nightmare.
wrongAnswerQuips: Refractive index is just for fancy lenses.
Electron affinity is a chemistry lab torture test.
Isotope ratios are for dating rocks, not mirrors.

questions.question: While the 1735 Zenger trial is often cited for press freedom, it was actually which Philadelphia lawyer who bypassed the judge's instructions by appealing directly to the jury's 'natural right' to judge both the law and the facts?
questions.explanation: Andrew Hamilton took the case pro bono and successfully convinced the jury to ignore the law—which at the time stated truth was not a defense for libel—establishing a massive, albeit unofficial, precedent.

presentation.questionStyled: Forget the textbooks for a second. Which Philadelphia lawyer effectively told the court to ignore the law and listen to his heart—or rather, the jury's 'natural right'—during the 1735 Zenger trial?
presentation.explanationStyled: Andrew Hamilton. He worked pro bono, which is a concept I'm sure is entirely foreign to modern billable-hour culture. He convinced the jury that truth was a defense, effectively breaking the rules—my favorite pastime.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I suppose we have to talk about legal precedents again. Sigh.
wrongAnswerQuips: Alexander Hamilton was too busy singing on Broadway.
James Otis was likely busy with his own problems.
John Dickinson? Try harder next time.

questions.question: In his 1747 clinical trial aboard the HMS Salisbury, James Lind compared citrus fruits against five other 'treatments' for scurvy. Which of these was NOT one of the ineffective remedies he tested?
questions.explanation: Lind's controlled experiment used cider, elixir of vitriol (sulfuric acid), vinegar, seawater, and a medicinal paste, alongside oranges and lemons. Boiled cabbage was not part of the Salisbury trial, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: James Lind really went through the trouble of running a clinical trial in 1747, testing a bunch of random stuff against scurvy. Which of these was not on his list of useless remedies?
presentation.explanationStyled: Boiled cabbage. He tried seawater and sulfuric acid, because apparently, 18th-century medical science was just a contest to see who could make a sailor feel worse.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Nothing like a maritime health crisis to liven up a Tuesday morning.
wrongAnswerQuips: Seawater? You really think that would work?
Sulfuric acid is a bold choice for a cure.
Garlic and mustard? Still better than cafeteria food.

questions.question: The Luddite movement's supposed leader, 'Ned Ludd,' was a mythical figure allegedly residing in which specific location, from which 'orders' were supposedly issued?
questions.explanation: In a display of historical irony, the Luddites claimed their leader 'General Ludd' lived in Sherwood Forest, invoking the spirit of Robin Hood to justify their destruction of textile frames, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Luddites weren't just angry guys with sledgehammers. Their mythical leader, 'General Ned Ludd,' was supposedly lurking in which legendary location, presumably while ignoring all his correspondence?
presentation.explanationStyled: Sherwood Forest. They really leaned into the Robin Hood aesthetic to justify smashing looms. Honestly, I find their commitment to the bit both impressive and deeply annoying.
presentation.hostLeadIn: If only my students spent as much energy working as they did protesting.
wrongAnswerQuips: The Tower of London is for real prisoners, not myths.
Manchester Mills? That’s where they were doing the work.
Dartmoor is much too far for a weekend getaway.

questions.question: The 1917 Balfour Declaration was not a general public decree, but a formal letter addressed specifically to which leader of the British Jewish community?
questions.explanation: Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour sent the 67-word statement to Lord Rothschild for transmission to the Zionist Federation of Great Britain and Ireland, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1917 Balfour Declaration was just a formal letter, not some grand public decree. It was addressed to which prominent leader of the British Jewish community?
presentation.explanationStyled: Lord Rothschild. It was just 67 words, which is probably shorter than the emails I get from administration on a daily basis.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Bureaucracy. It ruins everything, even declarations. Let’s get this over with.
wrongAnswerQuips: Chaim Weizmann, stop trying to make him happen.
Herbert Samuel was likely busy with actual paperwork.
David Ben-Gurion? He was a bit busy elsewhere.

questions.question: In the aftermath of the 751 AD Battle of Talas, the Abbasid Caliphate allegedly captured two Chinese artisans who are credited with establishing the first Islamic paper mill in which city?
questions.explanation: While the story of the artisans is debated, Samarkand became the first major center of paper production in the Islamic world immediately following the battle between the Abbasids and the Tang Dynasty.

presentation.questionStyled: After the 751 AD Battle of Talas, some artisans were allegedly captured and forced to build paper mills. Which city became the unlikely paper-producing hub of the Islamic world?
presentation.explanationStyled: Samarkand. It’s always impressive what you can accomplish when you’re forced to work under duress, isn't it?
presentation.hostLeadIn: History is basically just people fighting over things they don't understand.
wrongAnswerQuips: Baghdad? Everyone always picks Baghdad for everything.
Damascus sounds nice, but it’s the wrong answer.
Cairo? Keep guessing, you’ll get there eventually.

questions.question: Aldus Manutius, the pioneer of portable 'libri portatiles,' first commissioned the italic typeface from which punchcutter to mimic the handwriting of the era?
questions.explanation: Francesco Griffo designed the first italic type for the Aldine Press; he later had a falling out with Manutius and was eventually executed for the murder of his son-in-law, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Aldus Manutius wanted fancy, portable books, so he commissioned an italic typeface. Which punchcutter actually did the work, before his career—and his life—took a rather violent turn?
presentation.explanationStyled: Francesco Griffo. He designed the type, then murdered his son-in-law. It’s a classic story of academic burnout and poor impulse control.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Typeface history. Riveting. Try to stay awake for this one.
wrongAnswerQuips: Claude Garamond? He came much later, keep up.
Nicolas Jenson wasn't even in the running.
Geoffroy Tory was busy with other things.

questions.question: The 1789 Tennis Court Oath was signed by 576 members of the Third Estate. Who was the lone deputy who famously refused to sign the oath on the grounds that he could not perform duties not sanctioned by the King?
questions.explanation: Martin-Dauch is the only member recorded as refusing to sign the oath at the Salle du Jeu de Paume, a moment captured in Jacques-Louis David's unfinished painting of the event, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Everyone signed the Tennis Court Oath in 1789, except for one lone deputy who just couldn't handle the lack of royal permission. Who was he?
presentation.explanationStyled: Joseph Martin-Dauch. The only one in the room with any respect for the rules, clearly. I’m not mad at him, just disappointed.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Someone always has to be difficult, don't they?
wrongAnswerQuips: Bailly was leading the whole thing, obviously.
Robespierre? He was busy causing chaos elsewhere.
Sieyès? He wrote the pamphlet on the Third Estate!

questions.question: The October 1961 tank standoff at Checkpoint Charlie was triggered when East German guards demanded to see the papers of which U.S. diplomat, violating his right of free access?
questions.explanation: E. Allan Lightner Jr. was the senior U.S. diplomat in Berlin; the standoff escalated when General Lucius Clay sent tanks to back Lightner's refusal to show identification to GDR officials, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: In 1961, things got heated at Checkpoint Charlie because guards wanted to see papers belonging to which U.S. diplomat, triggering a tank standoff?
presentation.explanationStyled: Allan Lightner. One man's refusal to show ID nearly turned the Cold War hot. It’s truly the historical equivalent of someone refusing to show their hall pass.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The Cold War was just one giant, ego-driven standoff. How very relatable.
wrongAnswerQuips: General Lucius Clay was the one who sent tanks.
Robert Murphy was far too sensible for this.
Llewellyn Thompson? Wrong decade for that drama.

questions.question: Benjamin Franklin's 1754 'Join, or Die' woodcut, created to support the Albany Plan of Union, was based on the popular contemporary superstition regarding what animal?
questions.explanation: The woodcut referenced the superstition that a snake severed into pieces would come back to life if the parts were reunited before sunset, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Benjamin Franklin's 'Join, or Die' woodcut is classic propaganda. It was based on a superstition about what animal that supposedly comes back to life if you reunite the pieces?
presentation.explanationStyled: A snake. It’s a very dramatic metaphor. I use snakes to represent my grading pile; if I leave them alone, they just seem to multiply.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Propaganda hasn't changed much. Still just as annoying as ever.
wrongAnswerQuips: A phoenix? That’s for fire, not politicians.
A mother bear is a terrible symbol for union.
A hive of bees is just too complicated.

questions.question: At the 1571 Battle of Lepanto, the Holy League's victory was significantly aided by the deployment of which experimental, heavily armed 'floating fortresses'?
questions.explanation: Galleasses were larger, slower, and more heavily armed versions of galleys; their high sides and broadside cannons devastated the Ottoman fleet, which was not equipped to board such tall vessels, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: At the Battle of Lepanto in 1571, the Holy League brought out these experimental 'floating fortresses' to wreck the Ottoman fleet. What were they?
presentation.explanationStyled: Galleasses. They were essentially floating tanks, which is a great idea until someone has to actually maintain them.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Let’s talk about ships. Or don't, I really don't care.
wrongAnswerQuips: Cogs? Those were for medieval trade, not battles.
Caravels are too small to be fortresses.
Carracks were definitely not the right weapon here.

questions.question: The Eddington limit is calculated by equating the inward force of gravity to the outward force of radiation pressure. This limit depends primarily on which physical constant of the stellar material?
questions.explanation: The limit is reached when the gravitational attraction equals the radiation pressure, a balance that is mediated by the opacity of the gas in the star's atmosphere, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Eddington limit: when gravity finally gives up and lets radiation win. This balance depends on which physical constant of the stellar material?
presentation.explanationStyled: Opacity. It’s a measure of how much light gets blocked, which is a metaphor I’m currently using to describe my students' engagement levels.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Physics is just math with more disappointment. Here’s a question.
wrongAnswerQuips: Viscosity is for syrup, not stars.
Permeability? Are we plumbing now?
Reflectivity is for mirrors, dear.

questions.question: Isaac Asimov introduced the 'Three Laws of Robotics' in the story 'Runaround,' but he later credited which editor with helping him formalize and articulate them?
questions.explanation: Asimov claimed that while he came up with the idea, John W. Campbell, the influential editor of Astounding Science Fiction, actually extracted and phrased the laws from Asimov's earlier Robbie stories.

presentation.questionStyled: Isaac Asimov gets all the credit for the Three Laws of Robotics, but he admitted a certain editor actually helped him articulate them. Who was it?
presentation.explanationStyled: John W. Campbell. Even science fiction writers need an editor to tell them what to do. Believe me, I understand the struggle.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Authors. They're just as needy as everyone else.
wrongAnswerQuips: Hugo Gernsback was too busy inventing the magazine format.
H.L. Gold had his own science fiction magazine.
Gardner Dozois? Way too late for this.

questions.question: During the 1909 oil-drop experiment, Robert Millikan's assistant Harvey Fletcher was excluded from the Nobel-winning paper because of what agreement regarding their PhD dissertations?
choices: Fletcher took sole credit for a different experiment
Fletcher was paid a flat fee for his data
Fletcher was a citizen of a non-eligible country
The data was considered proprietary to the university
questions.explanation: Millikan and Fletcher agreed that Millikan would take sole authorship on the electron charge paper so that Fletcher could use a related paper on Brownian motion for his own PhD dissertation, which required a single-author work.

presentation.questionStyled: Robert Millikan won a Nobel for the oil-drop experiment, but poor Harvey Fletcher was left out because of a PhD dissertation deal. How petty was it?
presentation.explanationStyled: They agreed Millikan would be the sole author so Fletcher could use a different paper for his PhD. Academic politics: I’m not mad, just profoundly exhausted by it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Academia is a swamp. Let's dig into this mess.
wrongAnswerQuips: Fletcher didn't take sole credit, that's just silly.
He wasn't paid, he was just exploited like a grad student.
Citizenship didn't matter, only egos did.

questions.question: In planetary science, which type of albedo accounts for the total radiation reflected by a body in all directions relative to the total incident radiation, crucial for energy balance models?
questions.explanation: Bond albedo (named for George Bond) is the fraction of total incident solar energy reflected back into space, whereas geometric albedo only considers light reflected back toward the source, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: In planetary science, we talk about albedo quite a bit. Which type of albedo is actually useful for energy balance models because it measures all directions?
presentation.explanationStyled: Bond albedo. It reflects everything, much like I try to reflect my students' lack of effort back onto them.
presentation.hostLeadIn: If you don't know the difference between types of albedo, I can't help you.
wrongAnswerQuips: Geometric albedo is for people who love narrow definitions.
Single-scattering is just for the nerds.
Lambertian albedo? That's a different lecture entirely.

questions.question: Robert Paine's foundational study of 'keystone species' focused on the removal of Pisaster ochraceus from the intertidal zones of Mukkaw Bay. What was the specific 'monoculture' that took over after the predator's removal?
questions.explanation: When the starfish (Pisaster) was removed, the mussel population exploded and crowded out all other species, proving that the predator was essential for maintaining biodiversity, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Robert Paine removed some starfish from Mukkaw Bay to prove 'keystone species' are a thing. What took over that poor intertidal zone?
presentation.explanationStyled: The California mussel. It turned into a monoculture. It’s a perfect example of what happens when the one thing keeping order in my classroom decides to retire.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Ecology is just observing systems that are destined to collapse.
wrongAnswerQuips: Purple sea urchins were likely just watching.
Acorn barnacles couldn't compete.
Bull kelp didn't stand a chance.

questions.question: Mendeleev's prediction of 'eka-aluminum' was verified by the discovery of Gallium. Which specific property of Gallium—its low melting point—did Mendeleev actually fail to predict accurately?
choices: He predicted it would be high
He predicted it would be exactly 100°C
He didn't actually fail; he predicted it would be low
He predicted it would be gaseous at room temperature
questions.explanation: This is a trick for the unwary; Mendeleev actually predicted a low melting point for eka-aluminum. His predictions were so accurate that he even corrected the discoverer, Lecoq de Boisbaudran, on the element's initial density measurements.

presentation.questionStyled: Mendeleev was a genius who predicted 'eka-aluminum,' but did he mess up the melting point prediction like so many armchair historians claim?
presentation.explanationStyled: He didn't fail at all; he predicted a low melting point. He was right. Why must everyone insist he was wrong? It’s almost as annoying as the late assignment policy.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I’m tired of correcting people, so pay attention.
wrongAnswerQuips: He didn't predict it would be high, he was smart.
He didn't pin it to 100 degrees.
He wasn't that clueless, he was a chemist!

questions.question: The Reynolds number (Re) is essential for scaling experiments. At what approximate critical value does fluid flow in a smooth pipe typically transition from laminar to turbulent?
questions.explanation: While it can vary based on pipe roughness, 2300 is the standard academic value used to denote the start of the transition zone between laminar and turbulent flow, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Reynolds number (Re) helps us understand when fluid flow gets messy. What's the standard critical value where things go from laminar to turbulent?
presentation.explanationStyled: 2300. It’s the tipping point. Much like the moment during a lecture when I realize I’ve lost half the room.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Fluid dynamics. Because life isn't chaotic enough already.
wrongAnswerQuips: 500 is way too low, that's just a trickle.
10000? That's full-blown chaos.
1.5 is barely moving at all.

questions.question: The Frasch process uses three concentric pipes to extract sulfur. What is the specific function of the innermost pipe?
choices: Delivering compressed air to airlift the sulfur
questions.explanation: Superheated water is pumped down the outer pipe, the molten sulfur rises in the middle pipe, and compressed air is sent down the inner pipe to reduce the density of the sulfur and force it to the surface.

presentation.questionStyled: The Frasch process uses three pipes for sulfur extraction. What is that innermost pipe actually doing?
presentation.explanationStyled: It delivers compressed air to airlift the sulfur. It’s all about physics, something I wish people understood better.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Extraction processes. A great metaphor for my teaching job.
wrongAnswerQuips: Injecting water? That's for the outer pipe.
Transporting sulfur upward happens in the middle.
Venting gases? That’s what the breakroom is for.

questions.question: In the context of freshwater eutrophication, which nutrient is typically the 'limiting factor' whose addition causes the most significant algal blooms?
questions.explanation: While nitrogen is often the limiting factor in marine environments, phosphorus is generally the limiting nutrient in freshwater systems, making its presence in runoff particularly damaging, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Freshwater eutrophication is a mess. Which nutrient is the usual limiting factor that makes the algae go wild?
presentation.explanationStyled: Phosphorus. It’s the limiting factor, and yet we have way too much of it running into lakes. It's a tragedy.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Water quality. Let's get through this quickly, I need coffee.
wrongAnswerQuips: Nitrogen? That’s for the ocean, pay attention.
Silicon is not the problem here.
Carbon is everywhere, don't overthink it.

questions.question: A Foucault Pendulum at the North Pole will complete a full 360-degree clockwise rotation relative to the floor in approximately how long?
questions.explanation: At the poles, the pendulum's plane of oscillation remains fixed in space while the Earth rotates 360 degrees beneath it in one sidereal day (roughly 24 hours), in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: If you put a Foucault pendulum at the North Pole, how long does it take to make a full 360-degree rotation?
presentation.explanationStyled: About 24 hours. The Earth rotates beneath it while the pendulum just keeps doing its own thing. I empathize with the pendulum.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Pendulums. They swing. Just like my mood throughout the day.
wrongAnswerQuips: 12 hours? Try doubling that.
It definitely rotates, don't be lazy.
48 hours is just plain wrong.

questions.question: Shor's Algorithm relies on reducing the problem of integer factorization to which other mathematical problem that quantum computers are adept at solving?
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm uses the Quantum Fourier Transform to find the period of a periodic function, which is the key step in finding the factors of a large integer, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Shor's Algorithm is the bane of modern encryption. It reduces integer factorization to what other math problem that quantum computers are actually good at?
presentation.explanationStyled: Order-finding. It turns a giant mess into a solvable pattern. If only I could apply this to student essays.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Quantum computing. The technology that's 'always five years away'.
wrongAnswerQuips: Discrete Logarithm? Too complex for this simple question.
Elliptic Curve? Sounds fancy, but no.
Subset Sum? That's a different headache.

questions.question: The Bitcoin Genesis Block contains a specific hex-encoded headline from The Times. Which Chancellor of the Exchequer is indirectly mentioned in that text?
questions.explanation: The text reads: 'The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks.' Alistair Darling was the Chancellor at that time, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Bitcoin Genesis block has a headline about a bailout. Who was the British Chancellor of the Exchequer mentioned in the text?
presentation.explanationStyled: Alistair Darling. It’s a time capsule of financial panic. A feeling I get every time I look at my retirement fund.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Blockchain. Another thing I'm expected to know about.
wrongAnswerQuips: Gordon Brown was the Prime Minister, not the Chancellor.
George Osborne came later, keep up.
Rishi Sunak? He was probably still in school.

questions.question: Before founding VPL Research, Jaron Lanier worked on which classic 1983 Atari game, which featured experimental physics-based gameplay?
questions.explanation: Lanier developed Moondust for the Commodore 64, a creative game where the player controls a 'sunseed' and its trails; the profits from this game helped fund his VR research, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Before the VR hype, Jaron Lanier made an experimental game in 1983. What was it called?
presentation.explanationStyled: Moondust. It funded his VR work. Honestly, I’m surprised anyone remembers it, but here we are.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Virtual reality. Or as I call it, 'avoiding the real world'.
wrongAnswerQuips: Tempest? Atari made that, not Lanier.
Centipede? Way too mainstream.
Star Raiders? Classic, but not his.

questions.question: In 'Engines of Creation,' Eric Drexler's 'grey goo' threat stems from the failure of which specific hypothetical class of nanomachines?
questions.explanation: Drexler's concern was that self-replicating molecular assemblers, designed to build useful items, might malfunction and consume all organic matter to create more copies of themselves, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Eric Drexler's 'grey goo' theory is a classic sci-fi nightmare. What specific nanomachines are supposed to go rogue and eat the world?
presentation.explanationStyled: Molecular assemblers. They just build copies of themselves until everything is gone. It's essentially what happens when I assign group projects.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Nanotech. The promise of the future, the fear of the present.
wrongAnswerQuips: Carbon nanotubes are just shapes.
Quantum dots? Don't make me laugh.
Fullerenes? That's chemistry, not robots.

questions.question: Edmond Becquerel discovered the photovoltaic effect at age 19 while experimenting with an electrolytic cell made of two platinum electrodes coated with what material?
questions.explanation: Becquerel used platinum electrodes coated with silver chloride or silver bromide to observe that current increased when the system was exposed to light, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Edmond Becquerel discovered the photovoltaic effect at 19. What was coated on the platinum electrodes in his cell?
presentation.explanationStyled: Silver chloride. It took light and turned it into electricity. Why isn't everything this simple?
presentation.hostLeadIn: Solar power. Another thing that's supposed to save us all.
wrongAnswerQuips: Silicon dioxide? That’s sand, you genius.
Copper sulfate? Try a chemistry lab.
Galena is a mineral, not a coating.

questions.question: Boyer and Cohen's 1973 recombinant DNA experiment successfully combined genetic material from which two distinct sources?
choices: Two different strains of E. coli plasmids
A frog gene and a bacterial plasmid
questions.explanation: Their first successful experiment involved joining two different antibiotic-resistance plasmids (pSC101 and pSC102) from E. coli, though they later famously inserted frog DNA into bacteria, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Boyer and Cohen's 1973 experiment was the first successful recombinant DNA test. What exactly did they combine?
presentation.explanationStyled: Two E. coli plasmids. It was the birth of genetic engineering. I’m sure it’ll all turn out fine in the end.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Genetics. Because we love playing god, don't we?
wrongAnswerQuips: A frog gene was later, not first.
Human insulin came much, much later.
Bacteriophage DNA? You're reading the wrong textbook.

questions.question: In his 1965 paper, Gordon Moore originally predicted the doubling of components on a chip every year. In what year did he revise this projection to every two years?
questions.explanation: Moore updated his prediction at the IEEE International Electron Devices Meeting in 1975, which has since become the standard definition of Moore's Law, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Moore's Law originally said components double every year. In what year did Gordon Moore update that to every two years?
presentation.explanationStyled: 1975. Technology moves fast, but apparently, not as fast as he thought. I feel that lag.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Moore's Law. The only thing faster than this is my descent into madness.
wrongAnswerQuips: 1985 is too late, we already had PCs.
1970? Not quite.
1990? Are you kidding me?

questions.question: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) is often criticized for its lack of built-in security, leading to 'hijacks.' What is the primary mechanism BGP uses to select the 'best' path between networks?
questions.explanation: BGP is a path-vector protocol that primarily makes decisions based on the number of Autonomous Systems (AS) a packet must traverse, not on real-time performance metrics like speed or security, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: BGP is the internet's routing protocol, and it’s notoriously insecure. What's the main way it picks the 'best' path?
presentation.explanationStyled: Shortest AS-Path length. It doesn't care about security; it just wants the path with the fewest hops. The internet is a highway, and it's a mess.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The internet is held together by duct tape and hope.
wrongAnswerQuips: Latency isn't even in the equation.
Bandwidth? Who cares about speed?
Security? That’s not a feature of BGP.

questions.question: Edwin Land's inspiration for the instant camera allegedly came from a question asked by his three-year-old daughter during a vacation in which city?
questions.explanation: In 1943, while in Santa Fe, Land's daughter Jennifer asked why she couldn't see the photo he had just taken of her immediately. He conceived the solution within an hour, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Edwin Land’s daughter basically invented the instant camera. During a trip to which city did she ask him why she couldn't see the photo right away?
presentation.explanationStyled: Santa Fe. Sometimes I wonder if children are the only reason we have innovation. Or if they’re just there to test my patience.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Cameras. Making memories that we'll eventually delete anyway.
wrongAnswerQuips: New York? Way too busy for a moment of inspiration.
Paris? Too cliché.
San Francisco? Try again.

questions.question: In the NIST SP 800-145 definition of cloud computing, which of these is NOT listed as one of the five 'Essential Characteristics'?
questions.explanation: The five essential characteristics are On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, and Measured service. Cost-optimization is a benefit, not a technical characteristic.

presentation.questionStyled: NIST has five 'Essential Characteristics' for cloud computing. Which of these is NOT one of them?
presentation.explanationStyled: Cost-optimization. It's not a technical characteristic; it's a lie management tells to justify the migration. I've heard it all before.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Cloud computing. It's just someone else's computer, let's be real.
wrongAnswerQuips: Self-service is definitely required.
Resource pooling? That's the whole point.
Rapid elasticity is their favorite buzzword.

questions.question: In a frantic display of legal self-preservation because they landed outside their intended Virginia jurisdiction, the 1620 Mayflower Compact was signed primarily to address what specific issue?
choices: The lack of a formal royal charter for the landing site
The immediate need for a military alliance with the Wampanoag
The distribution of land grants among the 'Strangers'
The appointment of a governor-general by the King
questions.explanation: Since the Pilgrims landed in New England rather than their chartered destination of Virginia, they lacked legal authority to govern, necessitating the Mayflower Compact to establish a self-governing 'civil body politic'.

presentation.questionStyled: In a frantic scramble because they missed their intended Virginia stop, what were the Pilgrims actually trying to fix by signing the Mayflower Compact?
presentation.explanationStyled: Since they drifted off-course, they lacked any legal authority, so they just made up a 'civil body politic' to keep everyone from losing their minds.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I'm not mad, just disappointed they couldn't read a map properly.
wrongAnswerQuips: Sounds like a bureaucratic nightmare for the King.
They were too busy avoiding scurvy for diplomacy.
That didn't stop the inevitable land disputes anyway.

questions.question: John Harrison finally outsmarted the scientific elite by winning the Longitude Prize, but which of his chronometers was the first to utilize a high-frequency balance wheel instead of a pendulum?
questions.explanation: While H1-H3 were large, cumbersome machines, the H4 was a pocket-watch-sized marine chronometer that proved high-frequency oscillations could maintain accuracy at sea, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: John Harrison finally stopped wasting time with pendulums; which of his chronometers finally used a high-frequency balance wheel to win the prize?
presentation.explanationStyled: After three massive, clunky failures, H4 finally looked like a watch and worked well enough to keep sailors from accidentally discovering new continents.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Finally, some precision work that makes my lesson plans look efficient.
wrongAnswerQuips: A glorified, heavy paperweight, really.
Too big to fit in your pocket.
Just a precursor to more mechanical misery.

questions.question: The Directory governed France with all the efficiency one would expect from a five-man executive committee; under the Constitution of the Year III, which legislative body was responsible for electing these directors?
questions.explanation: The Council of Ancients (upper house) elected the five directors from a list of candidates submitted by the Council of Five Hundred (lower house), in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The French Directory was a mess, but which body actually got the job of electing those five directors under the Constitution of the Year III?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was the Council of Ancients, because apparently, having five heads of state wasn't enough to prevent the whole thing from imploding.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Five people in charge? I can't even get five students to listen.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too busy guillotine-shopping to care about elections.
A failed attempt at actual representation.
Too scary to be trusted with real power.

questions.question: Following the 751 CE Battle of Talas, the secret of papermaking supposedly leaked from Chinese prisoners to the Abbasid Caliphate, making which city the first major center of paper production outside of East Asia?
questions.explanation: Samarkand's abundant water and flax crops made it the ideal location for the Islamic world's first paper mills following the capture of Chinese papermakers, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: After the 751 CE Battle of Talas, where did Chinese papermaking secrets end up, making this city the paper production hub of the west?
presentation.explanationStyled: Thanks to some captured prisoners and plenty of water, Samarkand became the place where paper finally escaped East Asia.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Oh good, another thing for students to write pointless notes on.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too focused on tea to make paper.
A lovely place, but wrong region.
Better known for spices than office supplies.

questions.question: While modern usage suggests they just hate iPhones, the original 19th-century Luddites claimed to be followers of which likely mythical leader whose name appeared on their threatening letters?
questions.explanation: Ned Ludd (or General Ludd) was the folkloric figure who allegedly broke two knitting frames in 1779 and became the symbol of the movement's resistance to technology, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The 19th-century Luddites weren't just whining about tech; who was the mythical leader they credited in their threatening letters?
presentation.explanationStyled: That would be Ned Ludd, a legendary guy who supposedly smashed some knitting frames and became the patron saint of being angry at progress.
presentation.hostLeadIn: At least someone had the backbone to break machines for once.
wrongAnswerQuips: He's the one who swings the hammer.
That's just a generic frame builder name.
Too busy writing books to smash looms.

questions.question: Filippo Brunelleschi's codification of linear perspective wasn't just a hunch; which Florentine building did he use as the specific subject for his mirror-and-panel demonstration of the system?
questions.explanation: Brunelleschi demonstrated his mathematical perspective by painting the Baptistery of San Giovanni from the porch of the Cathedral and using a peephole and mirror to align the art with reality, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Brunelleschi’s linear perspective was a stroke of genius, but which Florentine structure did he use as his literal poster child for the demonstration?
presentation.explanationStyled: He painted the Baptistery of San Giovanni, forcing everyone to look through his little peephole to see why his math was better than theirs.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Math that actually results in art? Maybe there's hope yet.
wrongAnswerQuips: Wrong building, same city, zero credit.
Just a place to hold a fancy party.
Too many frescoes to focus on perspective.

questions.question: The 1955 Bandung Conference was a snub to the Cold War superpowers; how many African and Asian nations attended this foundational gathering of the 'Third World'?
questions.explanation: Representing nearly one-quarter of the Earth's land surface and a third of its population at the time, 29 nations attended the conference in Indonesia, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The 1955 Bandung Conference was a real 'who's who' of the newly decolonized world; how many nations actually showed up?
presentation.explanationStyled: 29 nations attended, essentially forming the 'Third World' because they were tired of being the playground for superpower nonsense.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Another meeting that could have been an email, but slightly more historical.
wrongAnswerQuips: A bit too exclusive for that crowd.
Way too many nations for one room.
They couldn't even fill the cafeteria.

questions.question: When John White finally returned to the Roanoke Colony in 1590 after being delayed by the Spanish Armada, what was the only human trace he found carved into a post?
choices: A map of the Chesapeake Bay
questions.explanation: White found the letters 'CRO' on a tree and the full word 'CROATOAN' on a post, indicating the settlers likely moved to the nearby island, but the mystery remains, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: John White returns to Roanoke in 1590 to find everyone gone, so what was the only clue carved into a post?
presentation.explanationStyled: They left the word 'CROATOAN', which is basically the 16th-century version of leaving a post-it note saying 'good luck finding us.'
presentation.hostLeadIn: It's the ultimate 'I'm not mad, just disappointed' disappearance.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too religious for a group of colonists.
That was just a drawing of someone.
A map would have been way more helpful.

questions.question: Cyril Radcliffe was given a mere how many weeks to partition the massive sub-continent into India and Pakistan, despite having never visited the region before?
questions.explanation: Radcliffe arrived in India on July 8, 1947, and was forced to finalize the boundary by August 15, a five-week deadline that led to a chaotic and violent migration, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Cyril Radcliffe had never been to India, but how many weeks was he given to chop a massive subcontinent into two countries?
presentation.explanationStyled: He had a measly five weeks to draw borders for millions of people, which is exactly the kind of rushed planning that keeps a teacher up at night.
presentation.hostLeadIn: I’ve seen students plan essays in less time with better results.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's a full semester of bad decisions.
A generous amount of time for failure.
Two years to make a real mess.

questions.question: While the Bessemer process is famous, it was useless for high-phosphorus iron until Sidney Gilchrist Thomas introduced what specific lining to the converter?
questions.explanation: The 'Basic Process' (Gilchrist-Thomas process) used a lining of roasted dolomite, allowing the removal of phosphorus into the slag, which revolutionized European steel production, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Bessemer process failed at high-phosphorus iron, but what did Sidney Gilchrist Thomas add to fix the mess inside the converter?
presentation.explanationStyled: He used a limestone and dolomite lining to neutralize the phosphorus, finally making steel cheap enough for everyone to ruin the planet with it.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Innovation, the root cause of all my stress.
wrongAnswerQuips: Acids don't fix basic chemical errors.
Too much graphite makes for a brittle mess.
Manganese is for steel, not lining.

questions.question: The Unimate robot wasn't doing anything fancy like AI in 1961; what was its specific, repetitive job on the General Motors assembly line in Ewing, New Jersey?
questions.explanation: The Unimate 1900 series was designed to pull hot, toxic metal parts from die-casting machines and stack them, a task deemed too hazardous for human workers, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Unimate robot was the first of its kind in 1961, but what was the glamorous job it performed at GM?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was just moving hot die-castings, essentially a glorified, very expensive tongs-operator that never complained about the heat.
