presentation.hostLeadIn: I wish I had a robot to handle my grading, but here we are.
wrongAnswerQuips: A bit too artistic for 1961.
That sounds like a job for a human.
Robots don't know how to tighten bolts.

questions.question: For a standard smooth-walled pipe, what is the approximate 'critical' Reynolds number where the transition from laminar to turbulent flow is typically expected to begin?
questions.explanation: While it can vary based on conditions, 2300 is the generally accepted dimensionless value where internal pipe flow transitions from laminar to turbulent, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Physics is just a series of annoyances; at what Reynolds number does fluid flow usually stop being laminar and start being chaotic?
presentation.explanationStyled: Once you hit 2300, the math stops being nice and starts being 'turbulent,' much like my classroom after lunch.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Fluid dynamics, because orderly flow is too much to ask for.
wrongAnswerQuips: Way too low for any real chaos.
That's still pretty calm, actually.
That's just pure, unadulterated noise.

questions.question: The reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone didn't just kill elk; it changed the physical geography of the park by altering what specific feature through a trophic cascade?
choices: The pH of the soil in the valleys
The meandering patterns of the rivers
The average elevation of the tree line
questions.explanation: By reducing elk grazing, bank-stabilizing vegetation grew back, which reduced soil erosion and fixed the paths of the rivers, making them meander less, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Wolves were brought back to Yellowstone, and they didn't just eat elk; what physical feature of the park did they reshape?
presentation.explanationStyled: They changed the meandering patterns of the rivers by letting vegetation grow back to hold the banks together. Biology is weird.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Changing the landscape just by existing? I wish I had that power.
wrongAnswerQuips: Wolves aren't really chemists, are they?
That's a bit of an environmental stretch.
Trees don't change because of fire.

questions.question: Gilbert N. Lewis is the patron saint of being overlooked; despite inventing the 'Octet Rule' and Lewis structures, how many times was he nominated for a Nobel Prize without ever winning?
questions.explanation: Lewis received 41 nominations for the Nobel Prize in Chemistry but never won, likely due to his difficult personality and disputes with colleagues, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Gilbert N. Lewis deserved better; how many times was this chemistry genius nominated for a Nobel Prize only to be rejected?
presentation.explanationStyled: He was nominated 41 times. Forty-one! I’ve had fewer people complain about my grading policy, and that's saying something.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Forty-one times. I'd have retired after the first three.
wrongAnswerQuips: A small number compared to reality.
He got it eventually, right? Wrong.
Thirty-one is just his lucky number.

questions.question: Masahiro Mori's 'Uncanny Valley' theory was first published in which year, long before modern CGI made us all feel slightly uneasy?
questions.explanation: Mori's essay 'Bukimi no Tani' was published in 1970, hypothesizing the drop in human affinity for near-human-like robots, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: When did Masahiro Mori decide that human-like robots were just creepy enough to name the 'Uncanny Valley'?
presentation.explanationStyled: He coined the term in 1970, back when robots were even clunkier and somehow still unsettling.
presentation.hostLeadIn: If you've seen a modern CGI character, you understand the feeling.
wrongAnswerQuips: A bit too early for modern robots.
That's when disco started to happen.
The nineties were for actual internet creepiness.

questions.question: The Coanda Effect, where a fluid jet clings to a curved surface, is named after Henri Coanda, who noticed it while testing a rudimentary jet aircraft in what year?
questions.explanation: Coanda observed the effect while testing his Coanda-1910 aircraft, noticing that the exhaust gases followed the shape of the fuselage rather than blowing straight back, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Henri Coanda noticed fluids clinging to curved surfaces in 1910; what was he actually doing when he stumbled upon this?
presentation.explanationStyled: He was testing his Coanda-1910 aircraft and realized his exhaust was hugging the fuselage, probably scaring him to death.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Accidental discoveries are the only way science ever moves forward.
wrongAnswerQuips: He didn't invent flight, just observed it.
Way too late for that kind of craft.
Too far in the future for his test.

questions.question: In the context of eutrophication, 'Liebig's Law of the Minimum' states that growth is controlled not by total resources, but by what?
questions.explanation: Liebig's Law states that the growth of an organism (like algae in a lake) is limited by the nutrient in shortest supply, often phosphorus in freshwater systems, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Liebig's Law is essentially the 'weakest link' of ecology; what determines how much growth happens in an ecosystem?
presentation.explanationStyled: It's controlled by the scarcest resource, like that one student who never shows up but somehow determines the pace of the whole class.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Sometimes I feel like I'm the limiting reagent in this room.
wrongAnswerQuips: The most abundant resource is just filler.
Averages are just another way to lie.
Toxicity is a byproduct, not a limiter.

questions.question: Before it was ever found on Earth in an ore of uranium, helium was first discovered in 1868 by observing a yellow spectral line in what?
questions.explanation: Pierre Janssen and Norman Lockyer independently observed a distinct yellow line in the sun's spectrum during a solar eclipse, leading to the naming of helium after 'Helios', in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Helium wasn't even found on Earth first; where was this gas spotted via spectral lines in 1868?
presentation.explanationStyled: It was identified in the solar chromosphere during an eclipse, which is just showing off if you ask me.
presentation.hostLeadIn: It took a literal star to find this gas. I can't even find a stapler.
wrongAnswerQuips: Comets are mostly ice and dirt.
Jupiter's atmosphere is too dense to bother.
Vesuvius is good for lava, not helium.

questions.question: Shakey the Robot wasn't just a wobbly box on wheels; it was the testbed for which ubiquitous pathfinding algorithm still used in game development and GPS today?
questions.explanation: The A* algorithm was developed in 1968 by Peter Hart, Nils Nilsson, and Bertram Raphael to help Shakey navigate around obstacles efficiently, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Shakey the Robot was the grandfather of modern AI; which pathfinding algorithm did it pioneer that still haunts game developers?
presentation.explanationStyled: The A* search algorithm, which still helps your video game characters find the door instead of walking into a wall for eternity.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Shakey had more direction in 1968 than most seniors have today.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's for trees, not robot movement.
Too slow for real-time navigation.
A classic, but for different math problems.

questions.question: To isolate just 0.1 grams of pure radium, Marie and Pierre Curie had to process approximately how many tons of the uranium ore pitchblende?
questions.explanation: It took roughly one ton of pitchblende (uraninite) residue to isolate a decigram of radium chloride, demonstrating the extreme difficulty of their task, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Curies were gluttons for punishment; how many tons of pitchblende did they process to get a tiny bit of radium?
presentation.explanationStyled: They had to churn through one ton of ore to get a tenth of a gram. My lab budget can't even cover a beaker.
presentation.hostLeadIn: One ton of rock for a speck of radioactive dust.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's enough to fill a small room.
Even ten tons wouldn't be enough effort.
A hundred tons is just plain excessive.

questions.question: Shor's Algorithm is terrifying for RSA encryption because it reduces the problem of integer factorization to which other mathematical problem that quantum computers solve efficiently?
questions.explanation: Shor's algorithm uses a quantum Fourier transform to solve the period-finding problem, which is the heavy lifting required to factorize large integers, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Shor’s Algorithm is the reason encryption is sweating; what mathematical problem does it solve to make integer factorization easy?
presentation.explanationStyled: It’s order-finding (period-finding), which sounds boring until you realize it effectively breaks modern security, so that's fun.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Mathematics: the most efficient way to ruin a security engineer's day.
wrongAnswerQuips: Discrete logs are a different kind of headache.
That's just simple arithmetic at work.
Too slow for any real decryption.

questions.question: If you dig into the Bitcoin Genesis Block, you'll find a secret message referring to a headline from which specific newspaper dated January 3, 2009?
questions.explanation: The message reads: 'The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks,' serving as both a timestamp and a political statement, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Bitcoin’s Genesis Block is a political manifesto; what newspaper headline is hidden in its very first lines?
presentation.explanationStyled: It cites The Times because apparently, the creator wanted to complain about bank bailouts in the blockchain forever.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Digital gold, because apparently fiat currency isn't cynical enough.
wrongAnswerQuips: That's too regional for a global ledger.
They prefer the more sensational tabloids.
A bit too financial for a revolutionary.

questions.question: PCR would be impossible without Taq polymerase; what specific extremophile bacterium found in Yellowstone's Mushroom Pool provides this heat-stable enzyme?
questions.explanation: Thermus aquaticus produces an enzyme that remains functional at the high temperatures needed to denature DNA during the PCR process, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: PCR is the only reason we know anything about genetics; which extremophile bacterium gave us the Taq polymerase enzyme?
presentation.explanationStyled: It's Thermus aquaticus, a little critter that enjoys hanging out in hot springs, which is a lot more productive than I am.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Extremophiles: nature's version of teachers dealing with administration.
wrongAnswerQuips: Good for digestion, not for PCR.
That's the one that survives radiation.
A little too cold for these reactions.

questions.question: In a P-N junction at equilibrium, what happens to the width of the depletion region when a reverse bias is applied to the diode?
questions.explanation: Reverse bias pulls majority carriers away from the junction, increasing the width of the depletion region and effectively stopping current flow, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Electronics are just organized lightning; what happens to the depletion region in a diode if you apply reverse bias?
presentation.explanationStyled: It widens, effectively blocking current and acting like the 'no' sign I put on my door during my lunch break.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Diode logic: the original boundary setting tool.
wrongAnswerQuips: It doesn't just disappear like my patience.
It should be static for better performance.
Oscillation is for faulty power supplies.

questions.question: Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants often use 'Solar Salt' for thermal storage; what is the typical chemical composition of this binary nitrate mix?
questions.explanation: The mixture of 60% sodium nitrate and 40% potassium nitrate is used because it has a high heat capacity and is stable up to 565°C.

presentation.questionStyled: Solar power needs storage, and they use 'Solar Salt'; what is the chemical composition of that nitrate mixture?
presentation.explanationStyled: It's 60% NaNO3 and 40% KNO3. It’s basically just fancy, heat-loving salt that stores energy because batteries aren't enough.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Storing heat is one thing, but storing energy is where it's at.
wrongAnswerQuips: Too salty for anything but the ocean.
Beryllium fluoride is far too toxic here.
Calcium mixtures don't handle the heat well.

questions.question: The Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) achieves atomic resolution by moving its tip with extreme precision using what type of electrical components?
questions.explanation: Piezoelectric materials change shape slightly in response to electric voltage, allowing the STM tip to be positioned with sub-nanometer accuracy, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The STM microscope is basically witchcraft at the atomic level; what components allow the tip to move with such precision?
presentation.explanationStyled: They use piezoelectric scanners, which change shape when you apply voltage. It’s the closest we get to moving atoms around like chess pieces.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Precise tools for a world that's becoming increasingly chaotic.
wrongAnswerQuips: Solenoids are too clunky for this scale.
Hydraulics are far too messy for microscopy.
Stepper motors have way too much backlash.

questions.question: While thousands of DNS servers exist, how many specific IP addresses are assigned to the 'Root Servers' that sit at the top of the internet's naming hierarchy?
questions.explanation: There are 13 logical root server IP addresses (a.root-servers.net through m.root-servers.net), though each address is served by hundreds of physical servers via anycast, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The internet naming hierarchy is simpler than it sounds; how many root server IP addresses actually manage the top of the DNS?
presentation.explanationStyled: There are 13 of them. Thirteen logical spots to hold the whole internet together. I’m amazed it stays online at all.
presentation.hostLeadIn: The internet is held together by thirteen IPs and duct tape.
wrongAnswerQuips: A lucky number, but wrong for DNS.
That's way more than we need today.
There's only one root, don't be ridiculous.

questions.question: Boyer and Cohen's first recombinant DNA experiment in 1973 used a plasmid from E. coli that carried resistance to which specific antibiotic?
questions.explanation: The pSC101 plasmid they used carried a gene for kanamycin resistance, allowing them to select for the bacteria that had successfully taken up the foreign DNA, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Boyer and Cohen started the biotech age with one specific plasmid in 1973; which antibiotic were they testing resistance against?
presentation.explanationStyled: They used Tetracycline to select their successes. It’s the classic 'did the bacteria take the plasmid' check, and it worked.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Recombinant DNA: changing life itself, one plasmid at a time.
wrongAnswerQuips: Penicillin is too old-school for this experiment.
Kanamycin is a common choice, but wrong here.
Streptomycin wasn't the target for pSC101.

questions.question: The Magellan NAV 1000 was a breakthrough in 1989, but how many satellites did the user have to wait for it to 'see' to obtain a single 2D position fix?
questions.explanation: At least three satellites are required for a 2D position (latitude and longitude), while four are needed for a 3D position (including altitude), in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: The Magellan NAV 1000 was a clunky handheld pioneer in 1989; how many satellites did it need for a 2D fix?
presentation.explanationStyled: You needed three. It was a simpler time when you could wait for a position update and still feel like an explorer.
presentation.hostLeadIn: GPS in the eighties: for when you wanted to be lost in style.
wrongAnswerQuips: Two is just a line, not a spot.
Four is for altitude, don't overachieve.
Five is overkill for an old device.

questions.question: In industrial 'Post-Combustion' Carbon Capture, what is the most common chemical class of solvents used to scrub CO2 from flue gases?
questions.explanation: Aqueous amine solutions (like Monoethanolamine) react with CO2 to remove it from the exhaust, then release the pure CO2 when heated in a regenerator, in case you were wondering.

presentation.questionStyled: Carbon capture is the industrial world's attempt at an apology; what solvents are most commonly used to scrub CO2?
presentation.explanationStyled: They use amines to grab the carbon from exhaust gases, proving that sometimes even chemistry can be a bit of a janitor.
presentation.hostLeadIn: Carbon capture: cleaning up after everyone else again.
wrongAnswerQuips: Esters are too busy being artificial fruit flavors.
Ketones are for science projects, not factories.
Silanes belong in rubber, not exhaust stacks.
